answer: Mudras (hastas) are codified hand gestures in Indian classical dance. Bharatanatyam and other forms use texts like the Abhinaya Darpana; each mudra carries meaning (e.g. pataka, tripataka) and combines with eyes and body for storytelling.

Bharatanatyam is a South Indian classical dance from Tamil Nadu, rooted in temple tradition. It features araimandi stance, intricate footwork (jatis), and abhinaya (expression), often to Carnatic music.

Find a teacher trained in an established style (e.g. Kalakshetra or other reputable lineages). Early training focuses on adavus, posture, and tala; later you add jatis and abhinaya. Carnatic music listening helps. Use NrityaNaad’s Academy Listing to discover schools near you.

Kathak is a North Indian form known for chakkars (spins), rhythmic footwork with tabla, and storytelling. Major gharanas include Jaipur, Lucknow, and Banaras, each with distinct emphasis on nrtta vs abhinaya.

Study with a qualified guru or an institution such as Kathak Kendra (Delhi) or a respected local academy. Expect years of work on tatkar, chakkars, thaat, and abhinaya; listen to tabla and Hindustani music. Check NrityaNaad’s Academy Listing for options in your city.
Kathakali is a Kerala dance-drama with elaborate makeup and costuming. It draws from Ramayana and Mahabharata episodes, with detailed facial and eye exercise and strong percussion support.
Odissi from Odisha is known for the tribhangi (three-bend) posture, lyrical torso movement, and sculpturesque poses inspired by temple reliefs.
A raga is a melodic framework in Indian classical music: specific notes, ornamentation (gamaka), and time-of-day or mood associations. Hindustani and Carnatic systems each have hundreds of ragas.
Tala is the rhythmic cycle in Indian music and dance (e.g. adi tala, teental). It is shown with hand gestures (kriyas) and underpins jatis and compositions.
Swaras are the solfege syllables Sa Ri Ga Ma Pa Da Ni (Carnatic) or Sa Re Ga Ma Pa Dha Ni (Hindustani). Learning swaras builds pitch and raga understanding.
The Natya Shastra, attributed to Bharata Muni, is an ancient treatise on dramaturgy, dance, and music. It classifies rasas, bhavas, and forms the theoretical base for much of Indian performance.
Carnatic music is the classical tradition of South India, centred on kriti compositions, ragas, talas, and improvisation (alapana, niraval, swara kalpana).
Hindustani classical music emphasises ragas, talas, and forms like khayal and dhrupad, with instrumental traditions (sitar, sarod, tabla) and strong gharana lineages.
Mohiniyattam is a graceful Kerala form, often lasya-oriented, with characteristic white-gold costume and swaying, subtle abhinaya.
Kuchipudi from Andhra Pradesh blends fast jatis, narrative, and sometimes theatrical elements; it shares roots with older dance-drama traditions of the region.
Manipuri dance from Manipur features fluid group patterns, devotional themes (often Vaishnava), and instruments like the pung (drum).
Sattriya is a classical form from Assam’s sattra monasteries, codified in the 20th century, with Ojapali influences and strict devotional repertoire.
